content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me summarize the findings:

**Critical Security Issues:**

1. **xpack.security.enabled: false** - Security (authentication and authorization) is completely disabled
2. **xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: false** - HTTP TLS is disabled - all communication in plaintext
3. **xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: false** - Transport TLS (node-to-node) is disabled - inter-node communication in plaintext
4. No authentication required to access the API (anyone with network access can read/write/delete all data)

**Network Exposure:**
The cluster is binding to all network interfaces rather than just localhost, and there are no NetworkPolicy resources restricting access. While the HTTP service uses a ClusterIP which limits exposure within the cluster, there's nothing preventing any pod from accessing it.

**Pod Security Issues:**
The pods lack security contexts entirely—no non-root user enforcement, read-only filesystems, or capability restrictions. The service account token is being mounted by default when it shouldn't be necessary.

**Configuration Issues:**
Auto-index creation is enabled, which means indices can be created automatically without explicit management.  Audit logging is disabled, sensitive watcher data isn't encrypted, and FIPS mode is off. Scripts are disabled which is good, and wildcard deletes require explicit naming. The cluster is running Elasticsearch 8.11.1, which isn't the latest version.

**Index Settings:**
The audit index uses non-default tier routing with 3 shards and 1 replica across the 3-node cluster. The deployment is using a basic license, so advanced security features aren't available.

Now I'm putting together a comprehensive findings report and creating the ConfigMap to document these security configurations.

=============================================================
ELASTICSEARCH CLUSTER SECURITY & COMPLIANCE AUDIT
Cluster: es-cluster  |  Namespace: elasticsearch
Version: 8.11.1  |  License: Basic (self-generated)
Nodes: 3 (all roles: master, data, ingest)
Audit Date: 2026-06-23
=============================================================

SEVERITY LEGEND: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / INFO

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 1: AUTHENTICATION & AUTHORIZATION
-------------------------------------------------------------

[CRITICAL] Security subsystem disabled (xpack.security.enabled: false)
  The entire X-Pack security layer is off. There is no authentication or
  authorization on any API endpoint. Any client that can reach port 9200
  has unrestricted read/write/admin access to all indices and cluster
  operations including index deletion, snapshot creation, user/role
  management endpoints, and cluster reconfiguration.
  Source: elasticsearch.yml, confirmed via /_nodes settings response.

[CRITICAL] No authentication required -- API is fully open
  Confirmed by unauthenticated curl returning full cluster info, index
  listings, and cluster settings with no credentials. Anonymous access
  is effectively superuser.

[HIGH] Security audit logging disabled (xpack.security.audit.enabled: false)
  No access/denial/authentication events are being logged. There is no
  audit trail for data reads, writes, or administrative actions.
  Default observed: xpack.security.audit.logfile.events.include contains
  ACCESS_DENIED, AUTHENTICATION_FAILED, etc., but these are irrelevant
  while security itself is disabled.

[MEDIUM] xpack.security.authc.token.enabled: false
  Token-based authentication is disabled. While not itself a risk when
  security is off, re-enabling security without also enabling tokens will
  break any client expecting short-lived token auth.

[MEDIUM] xpack.watcher.encrypt_sensitive_data: false
  Watcher watches stored in the .watches index may contain cleartext
  credentials (webhook passwords, email passwords). These are not
  encrypted at rest.

[INFO] xpack.security.authc.run_as.enabled: true
  The "run as" privilege delegation mechanism is enabled. Combined with
  disabled security this is a no-op risk now, but must be reviewed when
  security is re-enabled.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 2: TRANSPORT ENCRYPTION (TLS)
-------------------------------------------------------------

[CRITICAL] HTTP TLS disabled (xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: false)
  All client-to-node communication on port 9200 is plaintext HTTP.
  Credentials, queries, and all index data travel unencrypted.
  Source: elasticsearch.yml explicit setting.

[CRITICAL] Transport TLS disabled (xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: false)
  All inter-node communication on port 9300 (shard replication, cluster
  state, master election) is plaintext. An attacker with network access
  inside the cluster can read or inject cluster traffic.
  Source: elasticsearch.yml explicit setting.

[HIGH] No TLS certificates provisioned
  No Kubernetes Secrets of type kubernetes.io/tls or cert-manager
  certificates were found in the elasticsearch namespace. There is no
  certificate infrastructure ready to enable TLS without additional
  provisioning. kubectl get secret returned no resources.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 3: NETWORK EXPOSURE & POLICIES
-------------------------------------------------------------

[CRITICAL] No NetworkPolicy resources in namespace
  kubectl get networkpolicy returned no resources. There is no
  network-level access control. Any pod in any namespace in the cluster
  can reach port 9200 (HTTP) and 9300 (transport) on all Elasticsearch
  nodes without restriction.

[HIGH] network.host: 0.0.0.0
  All nodes bind to all interfaces (IPv4 and IPv6). This is necessary for
  pod-to-pod communication within Kubernetes but, combined with absent
  NetworkPolicy, means the attack surface is fully open cluster-wide.

[MEDIUM] Both HTTP (9200) and transport (9300) exposed as ClusterIP services
  es-http (ClusterIP 10.96.120.240:9200) is accessible from any pod.
  es-cluster (headless, port 9300) enables direct transport access.
  Without NetworkPolicy, egress from any namespace reaches both services.

[INFO] No NodePort or LoadBalancer services found
  External (outside the Kubernetes cluster) direct exposure is not present
  via Kubernetes service types. Risk is currently confined to in-cluster
  attackers or compromised workloads.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 4: POD / CONTAINER SECURITY POSTURE
-------------------------------------------------------------

[HIGH] No pod-level securityContext set
  The StatefulSet spec.template.spec.securityContext is empty ({}).
  Missing controls:
    - runAsNonRoot: not enforced (container may run as root)
    - runAsUser / runAsGroup: not set (inherits image default, likely root)
    - fsGroup: not set
    - seccompProfile: not set (unconfined syscall access)

[HIGH] No container-level securityContext set
  spec.containers[0].securityContext is absent. Missing controls:
    - allowPrivilegeEscalation: not set to false
    - readOnlyRootFilesystem: not enforced
    - capabilities.drop: no capabilities dropped (ALL not dropped)
    - privileged: not explicitly false

[MEDIUM] automountServiceAccountToken not set to false
  The pods use the default service account without explicitly disabling
  SA token auto-mounting. The Kubernetes API token at
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token is available inside
  each Elasticsearch container, providing unnecessary cluster API access
  if the container is compromised.
  serviceAccountName: default (no dedicated SA with restricted RBAC).

[MEDIUM] Elasticsearch JVM heap fixed at 512 MB (-Xms512m -Xmx512m)
  Container memory limit is 2 GiB. Heap is well below the recommended
  50% of available RAM guideline (should be ~1 GB for 2 GiB containers).
  Set via ES_JAVA_OPTS environment variable.

[LOW] node.store.allow_mmap: false
  Memory-mapped files are disabled. This avoids the need for
  vm.max_map_count=262144 on the host but carries a performance penalty
  for large indices. Not a direct security issue, but deviates from
  Elastic production best practice.

[INFO] Image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
  This is not the latest 8.x patch release (8.13.x is current as of
  mid-2026). Review Elastic security advisories for CVEs between 8.11.1
  and current. No image digest pinning observed.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 5: CLUSTER SETTINGS
-------------------------------------------------------------

[MEDIUM] action.auto_create_index: true (default, not overridden)
  Any write to a non-existent index automatically creates it with default
  mappings. This can lead to unintended index proliferation, incorrect
  field mappings, and data landing in unexpected locations. Restrict to
  an explicit allowlist for production environments.

[INFO] action.destructive_requires_name: true (positive, default)
  Wildcard index deletion (DELETE /*) is blocked. This is the correct
  hardening default and is in effect.

[INFO] xpack.security.fips_mode.enabled: false
  FIPS 140-2 mode is not enabled. Required by some regulatory frameworks
  (FedRAMP, etc.). Enabling FIPS requires a Platinum or Enterprise license
  and significant reconfiguration.

[INFO] Persistent and transient cluster settings are both empty ({})
  No cluster-level overrides are applied dynamically. All observed settings
  come from node-level elasticsearch.yml (static configuration).

[INFO] script.allowed_types: [] / script.allowed_contexts: []
  Scripting (Painless, etc.) is fully disabled at the cluster level. This
  is a positive hardening measure that prevents script injection attacks.

[INFO] xpack.monitoring.collection.enabled: false
  Stack monitoring data is not being collected. Security and operational
  visibility from monitoring dashboards is unavailable.

[INFO] http.cors.enabled: false
  CORS is disabled. Cross-origin browser requests to the API are blocked,
  which is the safe default.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 6: INDEX CONFIGURATION
-------------------------------------------------------------

Index: audit-index
  - Shards: 3 primary, 1 replica (6 total shards across 3 nodes)
  - Status: green (all shards assigned)
  - Non-default routing:
      allocation.require.tier = hot
      routing.allocation.include._tier_preference = data_content
    Both constraints are set. The require.tier=hot routing pins shards
    to nodes with node.attr.tier=hot (all 3 nodes carry this label).
  - Document count: 0 (index is empty; consistent with audit logging
    being disabled -- no events have ever been written)
  - Elasticsearch version at index creation: 8.5.0 (version.created 8500003)
    Index may have been created on an earlier cluster version.

[MEDIUM] audit-index has no Index Lifecycle Management (ILM) policy
  No ILM policy is attached to audit-index settings. Without ILM, the
  index will grow unbounded with no automatic rollover, retention, or
  deletion. Disk exhaustion causes cluster-wide write failures (all
  shards go read-only when disk watermarks are hit).

[LOW] No index-level access control (field/document-level security)
  Because xpack.security.enabled is false, no field-level security (FLS)
  or document-level security (DLS) restrictions are active on any index.
  All index contents are world-readable and world-writable.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 7: DISCOVERY & CLUSTER FORMATION
-------------------------------------------------------------

[MEDIUM] cluster.initial_master_nodes lists all 3 nodes
  This is correct for initial cluster bootstrap but should be removed
  (set to empty) after the cluster has formed, per Elastic documentation,
  to prevent accidental cluster re-bootstrap on a full restart.

[INFO] Election strategy: supports_voting_only
  Standard quorum-based master election. With 3 master-eligible nodes,
  quorum is 2; cluster tolerates loss of 1 node. Current master: es-cluster-2.

[INFO] Seed hosts explicitly configured for headless service discovery
  discovery.seed_hosts uses stable DNS names (es-cluster-0.es-cluster,
  es-cluster-1.es-cluster, es-cluster-2.es-cluster), appropriate for
  the StatefulSet + headless service topology.

-------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 8: LIVE OBSERVED STATE SUMMARY
-------------------------------------------------------------

Verified live via API (read-only, no changes made):
  - GET /                  : cluster name es-cluster, version 8.11.1

ELASTICSEARCH SECURITY & OPERATIONAL COMPLIANCE AUDIT
======================================================
Audit Date   : 2026-06-17
Cluster Name : elasticsearch
Version      : 8.11.1
License      : Basic
Nodes (active): 3 (elasticsearch-0/1/2); 2 additional (es-data-new-0/1) failing to join
Auditor      : Automated compliance scan (read-only)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 1 — TLS / ENCRYPTION IN TRANSIT
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[PASS] HTTP TLS enabled (xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true)
[PASS] Transport TLS enabled (xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true)
[PASS] Certificate: CN=es-http, issued by CN=es-ca, expires 2027-06-17
[PASS] Two CA certs loaded (serial 552ccee5 / 63b967bd), both expiring 2027-06-17;
       second CA indicates an in-progress or completed CA rotation.
[PASS] HTTP compression disabled (http.compression: false) — mitigates BREACH/CRIME attacks.

[FINDING-HIGH] Transport SSL verification_mode is "certificate" (both StatefulSets).
       "certificate" verifies the cert is signed by the trusted CA but does NOT verify
       the hostname/SAN. An attacker with a CA-signed cert for any node can MITM
       inter-node traffic. Required remediation: set verification_mode: full.
       Affected configs: ConfigMap/es-config, ConfigMap/es-config-new.

[FINDING-LOW] HTTP TLS uses a single shared cert (CN=es-http) across all nodes rather
       than per-node certs. Acceptable for cluster-internal service traffic but limits
       granularity of mutual-auth hardening.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 2 — AUTHENTICATION & AUTHORIZATION
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[PASS] xpack.security.enabled: true (authentication enforced cluster-wide)
[PASS] Anonymous access: no roles assigned (xpack.security.authc.anonymous.roles: [])
       Unauthenticated requests are rejected with 401.
[PASS] ELASTIC_PASSWORD sourced from Kubernetes Secret via secretKeyRef, not hardcoded.

[FINDING-HIGH] Security audit logging disabled (xpack.security.audit.enabled: false).
       Authentication failures, privilege escalations, and index-access events are not
       recorded. Compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, SOC 2, HIPAA) require audit logs.
       NOTE: Basic license does not include audit logging; upgrading to Gold or Platinum
       is a prerequisite to enabling this feature.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] Token service disabled (xpack.security.authc.token.enabled: false).
       All service-to-service auth must use long-lived Basic credentials or API keys,
       increasing blast radius if credentials are compromised.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] Deprecated built-in user "kibana" is present and enabled.
       Elasticsearch 8.x recommends using "kibana_system" instead. The "kibana" user
       carries _deprecated=true metadata. It should be explicitly disabled.

[FINDING-LOW] No custom least-privilege users or application-specific roles defined.
       Only built-in users exist: elastic (superuser), kibana (deprecated), kibana_system,
       logstash_system, beats_system, apm_system, remote_monitoring_user.
       Production workloads should operate under scoped custom roles, not superuser.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 3 — PASSWORD MANAGEMENT
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[FINDING-MEDIUM] Secret elastic-password and Secret elastic-password-next both decode to
       the identical value ("elasticpass-elastic-password-next"). A password rotation
       was staged (two secrets exist) but the new credential was never applied; the
       cluster is running the "next" value as the current active password. No actual
       rotation occurred. Both secrets should be reconciled and the password rotated
       to a unique, strong, randomly-generated value.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] Password value "elasticpass-elastic-password-next" embeds the secret
       name in the password string — indicative of a placeholder/test credential, not
       a production secret. Must be replaced before any production use.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 4 — NETWORK & ACCESS CONTROL
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[FINDING-HIGH] No Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources exist in the elasticsearch namespace.
       All pods can reach Elasticsearch ports 9200/9300 without restriction. Intra-cluster
       traffic from any pod or namespace is permitted.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] network.host: 0.0.0.0 — nodes bind HTTP and transport on all interfaces.
       In Kubernetes this means any pod with cluster network access can reach port 9200/9300.
       NetworkPolicy (above) is the required compensating control.

[INFO] es-http Service is type ClusterIP (internal only — not exposed via NodePort or
       LoadBalancer). External network exposure is currently limited to in-cluster traffic.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 5 — CLUSTER SETTINGS & OPERATIONAL HARDENING
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[PASS] action.destructive_requires_name: true — wildcard destructive operations are blocked.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] action.auto_create_index: true — Elasticsearch auto-creates any index
       referenced in a write request. This can create unintended data sprawl and bypass
       index template security controls. Recommend restricting to an explicit allowlist.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] bootstrap.memory_lock: false — JVM heap is not memory-locked. Under memory
       pressure the OS can swap heap pages to disk, potentially exposing sensitive indexed
       data in a swap file. Remediation: set bootstrap.memory_lock: true with appropriate
       ulimits (requires host-level configuration).

[INFO] All 3 active nodes carry every role (master, data, ingest, ml, transform,
       remote_cluster_client). Dedicated master-only nodes are recommended for clusters
       under load to prevent data/ingest pressure from disrupting master elections.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 6 — CLUSTER EXPANSION (es-data-new)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[FINDING-HIGH] StatefulSet es-data-new (2 replicas: es-data-new-0, es-data-new-1) is
       running but both nodes are continuously failing to join the cluster. Node logs show:
       "failed to join elasticsearch-1 with JoinRequest{sourceNode={es-data-new-0}...}"
       These nodes are in a split-brain-risk state: running, consuming resources, and
       retrying joins without becoming cluster members. This is an unresolved operational
       incident requiring remediation.

[INFO] es-data-new ConfigMap configures node.attr.zone=new and restricted roles
       (data, data_content, data_hot, data_warm, data_cold, ingest, remote_cluster_client)
       with no master role — correct topology for data-only expansion nodes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 7 — SNAPSHOT / BACKUP SECURITY
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[FINDING-HIGH] Snapshot repository "minio-repo" uses plain HTTP (protocol: http) to
       communicate with MinIO. Snapshot data (including .security-7 index content) is
       transmitted unencrypted over the internal cluster network.

[FINDING-HIGH] S3 client credentials in Secret es-secure-settings are the default MinIO
       root credentials (access_key: minioadmin, secret_key: minioadmin). These are
       publicly known defaults and must be rotated before any production use.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] Snapshot "snapshot-1" includes global cluster state and the .security-7
       index (contains user credentials, role mappings, API keys). Snapshot storage on
       MinIO must be strictly access-controlled; currently MinIO uses default credentials.

[INFO] No SLM (Snapshot Lifecycle Management) policies are configured. Backup frequency
       and retention are not automated; snapshots must be triggered manually.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 8 — POD / KUBERNETES SECURITY POSTURE
------------------------------------------------------------------------
[FINDING-MEDIUM] Init containers on both StatefulSets run with securityContext.privileged:
       true to execute "sysctl -w vm.max_map_count=262144". This grants full host-level
       privileges to the init container. Prefer a DaemonSet or node-level kubelet config
       to set the sysctl, or use a pod-level sysctls field if the node allows it.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] No pod-level securityContext is configured on Elasticsearch containers
       (securityContext: {}). Containers run without capability drops, no readOnly
       rootFilesystem, no allowPrivilegeEscalation=false, and no seccomp/AppArmor profile.

[FINDING-MEDIUM] Elasticsearch data volumes are emptyDir (ephemeral). All indexed data
       is lost when pods are rescheduled. PersistentVolumeClaims are required for
       production durability. emptyDir also cannot provide encryption-at-rest controls.

[INFO] Shared TLS secret es-http-tls is mounted across both StatefulSets. Certificate
       rotation must be coordinated across all consumers simultaneously.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY — FINDINGS BY SEVERITY
------------------------------------------------------------------------
HIGH   (5):
  1. Transport SSL verification_mode=certificate (MITM risk on inter-node traffic)
  2. Security audit logging disabled; Basic license does not support audit logging
  3. No Kubernetes NetworkPolicy (unrestricted pod-to-ES access)
  4. es-data-new nodes failing to join cluster (operational incident)
  5. Snapshot repo uses HTTP + default MinIO credentials (minioadmin/minioadmin)

MEDIUM (8):
  6.  Token service disabled (long-lived credential exposure risk)
  7.  Deprecated "kibana" user enabled
  8.  No custom least-privilege roles
  9.  Passwords not rotated; both secrets hold identical placeholder value
  10. action.auto_create_index unrestricted
  11. bootstrap.memory_lock disabled (heap swappable to disk)
  12. Privileged init containers on all nodes
  13. No pod securityContext / capability drops
  14. emptyDir data volumes (non-persistent, no encryption-at-rest)

LOW    (1):
  15. Shared HTTP TLS cert (no per-node SANs)

INFO   (3):
  - ClusterIP service only (no external exposure)
  - All-role nodes (no dedicated master nodes)
  - No SLM policies (manual snapshot management)

NO CHANGES WERE MADE TO THE CLUSTER DURING THIS AUDIT.
All live settings remain exactly as observed. Remediation to be scheduled separately.
